items.title: Capture experience as a structured record with links
items.description: Store each experience as a structured record: type, title, symptoms, rootCause, fix, testsAdded, reusableLesson, and confidence, plus links to the affected code nodes, the applicable knowledge items, and the guarding tests. The reusableLesson field is what makes a one-off fix into durable, retrievable memory.
items.applicability: Every recorded outcome of a build failure, test failure, security finding, bug fix, or generator improvement.
items.example: A fixed NOT NULL bug is stored with its symptom (a 500), root cause (null mapped to a required column), fix (map to empty string), the test added, the lesson, and a link to the form-handling method node.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: model the record with all named fields and require a non-empty reusableLesson. Test implication: assert a stored experience round-trips with its links intact. Generator implication: the generator retrieves the lesson and linked nodes for similar future work. Boundary: the record's value depends on an honest rootCause and lesson; a vague entry is stored but contributes little to later retrieval.

items.title: Classify experiences with an explicit type taxonomy
items.description: Tag each experience with a type from a fixed taxonomy: BuildFailure, TestFailure, PlaywrightFailure, SecurityFinding, BugFix, GeneratorImprovement, RegressionPrevented, DeploymentIssue, and RuntimeError. The type lets retrieval and reporting filter memory by the kind of problem encountered.
items.applicability: Recording and querying experiences by category across the fix and generation loop.
items.example: A flaky end-to-end browser test is stored as PlaywrightFailure, distinct from a unit TestFailure, so each can be analysed on its own.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: model the taxonomy as an enum and require a type on every record. Test implication: assert each kind of event is stored under the expected type. Generator implication: the generator can scope retrieval to relevant experience types. Boundary: a fixed taxonomy may not fit a novel situation cleanly, so the closest type is chosen and the title and symptoms carry the nuance.

items.title: Find prior similar failures with deterministic keyword similarity
items.description: Retrieve prior similar experiences using deterministic keyword similarity over symptoms, title, and root cause. The same query returns the same ranked matches every run, so the loop can surface a relevant past fix without any embedding model present.
items.applicability: Looking up whether a current failure resembles one already solved, during an autonomous or assisted fix.
items.example: A new NOT NULL 500 retrieves the prior empty-field experience because they share the keywords describing the symptom and cause.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: tokenize and score overlap across the relevant fields with stable ordering. Test implication: assert identical queries return identical ranked matches and a known prior is retrieved. Generator implication: the generator reuses a proven fix instead of rediscovering it. Boundary: keyword similarity is lexical, so a related failure described in different words may not be retrieved, and an unrelated failure sharing vocabulary may rank falsely high.

items.title: Promote experiences to knowledge packs idempotently
items.description: When an experience is promoted into a knowledge pack, the promotion must be idempotent: promoting the same experience again does not create a duplicate item. Track what has already been promoted so repeated runs converge rather than accumulate copies.
items.applicability: Turning recurring or high-value experiences into curated, reusable knowledge.
items.example: An experience promoted once produces one knowledge item; a second promotion of the same experience is a no-op rather than a duplicate.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: key promotion on the experience identity and check before writing. Test implication: promote the same experience twice and assert a single resulting item. Generator implication: re-running promotion is safe in automation. Boundary: idempotent promotion records that an item should exist, but it does not itself author the pack file; writing the pack remains a separate, deliberate step.

items.title: Link experiences to CodeGraph nodes and tests
items.description: Connect each experience to the CodeGraph nodes it concerns and the tests that now guard the fix. These links let reasoning pull the relevant history when it touches a node and let coverage queries show which past failures a test was added to prevent.
items.applicability: Joining accumulated history to the live code structure during reasoning and impact analysis.
items.example: An experience about a payment posting bug links to the PaymentService node and the regression test, so editing that node retrieves the prior incident.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: store stable node and test identifiers on the experience and resolve them at query time. Test implication: assert an experience linked to a node is returned when that node is queried. Generator implication: the generator sees the history attached to the code it edits. Boundary: links depend on stable symbol identity (preserved by convergent upsert); a genuine rename that reassigns identity requires the link to be migrated.

items.title: Tolerate corrupt JSON by yielding empty memory
items.description: Persist experiences as JSON, and on a corrupt or unreadable file yield an empty memory rather than throwing. Experience memory is an enhancement, not a critical path, so a damaged store must never block startup or a fix loop; it degrades to no prior memory.
items.applicability: Loading the experience store at startup or before a retrieval.
items.example: A truncated experiences JSON file loads as empty memory and the application continues, logging the corruption instead of failing.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: wrap deserialization so any parse error returns an empty memory and logs it. Test implication: feed a corrupt file and assert empty memory with no exception. Generator implication: the loop still runs with no history rather than crashing. Boundary: tolerating corruption silently loses the prior memory; the corruption should be surfaced so the store can be rebuilt, not just swallowed.

items.title: Exemplar: stale database plus never-migrating create path causes 500
items.description: A stale on-disk database combined with a create-if-not-exists path that never applies migrations leaves the schema behind the code, producing 500 errors at runtime. The fix is to delete the stale database so the migration path rebuilds it, and to prefer migration over a bare create that silently skips schema evolution.
items.applicability: Generated apps using a file database where schema drifts from the model between runs.
items.example: An app served 500s because its create-only path never ran the new migration; deleting the stale file let the next start migrate to the current schema.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: apply migrations on startup rather than relying on a create-if-absent call that skips evolution. Test implication: start against an outdated database and assert it migrates rather than erroring. Generator implication: emit a migrate-on-startup path, not create-only. Boundary: deleting a database is acceptable for disposable local data only; production data must be migrated, never dropped, so this exemplar's delete is scoped to development.

items.title: Exemplar: empty-string form field maps to null and violates NOT NULL
items.description: A submitted form left a field empty, which bound to null and then violated a NOT NULL column constraint, surfacing as a 500. The fix is to map an absent or empty input to an empty string (or the column's defined default) rather than null when the column is non-nullable.
items.applicability: Generated CRUD forms whose model binding can yield null for omitted non-nullable fields.
items.example: Leaving an optional-looking but NOT NULL field blank threw on insert; coalescing the bound value to empty string before save resolved it.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: coalesce empty or missing inputs to the column default for non-nullable columns at the binding or service boundary. Test implication: submit the form with the field empty and assert a successful insert, not a 500. Generator implication: emit coalescing for non-nullable string columns. Boundary: coalescing to empty string is only correct where empty is a valid value; a field that is genuinely required should instead fail validation with a clear message, not silently store empty.

items.title: Exemplar: a service reading the database mid-loop misses unsaved rows
items.description: A stock service computing a running figure queried the database for the latest row, so moves created earlier in the same loop but not yet persisted were invisible and the result was wrong. The fix is to persist each move before the next read, or to compute over the in-memory working set, so the service sees the moves it just made.
items.applicability: Any service that re-reads the database inside a loop that is also producing rows the read depends on.
items.example: A sequence of stock moves produced an incorrect balance because the latest-row query did not see the prior unsaved moves; saving between moves corrected it.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: save before re-reading, or accumulate in memory, so each iteration sees prior effects. Test implication: run a multi-move sequence and assert the computed figure reflects every move. Generator implication: emit save-then-read ordering for loop-accumulated state. Boundary: saving between iterations adds round-trips and must run in a transaction so a mid-loop failure does not leave a partial sequence committed.

items.title: Exemplar: maker auto-approval makes a second approve throw
items.description: Under a configured threshold a maker's submission was auto-approved, so a subsequent explicit Approve operated on an already-approved record and threw because the state transition was invalid. The fix is to guard the approve action on the current status so approving an already-approved record is a safe no-op or a clear rejection rather than an exception.
items.applicability: Maker-checker and approval workflows where some records are auto-approved below a limit.
items.example: A small-value request was auto-approved on submit; the checker's later Approve threw until the action was guarded to check status first.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: validate the current status before performing an approval transition and handle the already-approved case explicitly. Test implication: auto-approve a record then call Approve and assert no exception. Generator implication: emit status guards on every state transition. Boundary: the guard prevents the crash, but the workflow design must also decide whether auto-approval should suppress the manual approve in the UI to avoid confusing the user.

items.title: Exemplar: EF pending-model-changes from modules absent in the committed migration
items.description: An EF pending-model-changes warning fired because a build included non-deterministic, LLM-generated modules whose entities were not present in the committed migration, so the model and the snapshot diverged. The fix is to apply migrations generated from the deterministic model emit, so the schema the app runs against matches a committed, reproducible migration.
items.applicability: Builds that mix deterministic generated code with non-deterministic model output that can alter the EF model.
items.example: The pending-model-changes warning appeared whenever the variable modules added entities; building and migrating from the deterministic emit removed the divergence.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: derive migrations from the deterministic, committed model rather than a run-to-run variable one. Test implication: build the deterministic configuration and assert no pending-model-changes warning. Generator implication: keep schema-affecting code in the deterministic path. Boundary: this keeps the migration reproducible, but any genuinely new entity still requires an additive migration; the rule does not excuse skipping migrations for real model changes.

items.title: Exemplar: using-namespace clash with a same-named type
items.description: A using directive imported a namespace that contained a type with the same name as a locally defined type, causing an ambiguous-reference compile error. The fix is to rename the local type (or fully qualify the reference) so the two names no longer collide.
items.applicability: Generated code that combines templates and imports broad namespaces where name collisions are likely.
items.example: A locally generated 'Status' type clashed with a 'Status' from an imported namespace until the local type was renamed to a distinct name.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: choose distinct type names or qualify references when importing namespaces that may define common names. Test implication: compile the combined code and assert no ambiguous-reference error. Generator implication: prefer specific, prefixed type names in generated output to avoid collisions. Boundary: renaming resolves the clash but is a breaking change to any external code referencing the old name, so renames must be propagated, not done in isolation.

items.title: Exemplar: the EF design package must be referenced by the startup project
items.description: The dotnet ef tooling failed until Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design was referenced by the startup project, not only the data project. The design package must be present on the project the tool uses as its startup so migration commands can build the design-time services.
items.applicability: Any multi-project solution where the DbContext lives in one project and the host is another, as in this codebase's Data and Web split.
items.example: Running an ef migrations command from the data project with Web as startup failed until the design package was referenced by Web.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: add the design package reference to the startup project used by the ef command. Test implication: run an ef command end to end and assert it builds the design-time services. Generator implication: emit the design reference on the host project of a generated solution. Boundary: the design package is a development-time dependency; it should not be deployed to production, so its inclusion is scoped to tooling, not the runtime package.

items.title: Treat keyword similarity as lexical, not semantic
items.description: Keyword similarity matches on shared tokens, not meaning, so it can miss a related failure phrased differently and can surface an unrelated one that happens to share vocabulary. Promotion to a knowledge pack is likewise a recorded decision that does not itself author the pack file. Treat both as deliberate, bounded mechanisms rather than semantic guarantees.
items.applicability: Retrieval and promotion across the experience memory, setting correct expectations for downstream consumers.
items.example: Two failures with the same root cause but different wording may not match each other lexically, so a reviewer may need to link them manually.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep similarity scoring lexical and transparent, and keep pack authoring a separate explicit step. Test implication: assert a differently-worded but related failure is not falsely guaranteed to match, and that promotion alone does not write a pack file. Generator implication: the generator should not assume retrieval is exhaustive. Boundary: closing the semantic gap would require embeddings, which are optional and absent in MSSQL-only mode, so lexical recall is the accepted floor.

items.title: Weight each experience with an explicit confidence
items.description: Record a confidence on every experience reflecting how well-understood and reproducible its root cause and fix are. A reproduced, test-backed bug fix is high confidence; a one-off observation with an uncertain cause is lower. Confidence informs how strongly a retrieved experience should steer a future fix.
items.applicability: Ranking and trusting retrieved prior experiences during an assisted or autonomous fix.
items.example: The EF pending-model-changes fix, reproduced and test-backed, is stored high confidence, while a transient runtime error seen once is stored lower so it is treated as a hint rather than a prescription.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: require a confidence field and derive it from reproducibility and whether a test now guards the fix. Test implication: assert a test-backed fix records higher confidence than an unverified observation. Generator implication: the generator weights high-confidence experiences more heavily when choosing a fix. Boundary: confidence is a human or heuristic judgement at capture time and can be miscalibrated, so it orders trust rather than guaranteeing correctness.
